title: When Training Never Settles Down
concept: Unlike typical supervised training that converges to a stable, low-loss state, GAN training can oscillate indefinitely — generator and discriminator quality cycling up and down without ever settling.

exercises.prompt: In most supervised learning (like a simple image classifier), what does a 'well-behaved' loss curve typically do as training goes on?

options.text: It decreases and then levels off, settling near a low value
It oscillates up and down forever
It resets to zero every epoch

exercises.explain: Right — most training loss curves converge: they drop and flatten out. GANs often don't play by that rule.

cards.title: The finish line that keeps moving away
cards.body: Train a normal classifier long enough and its loss curve settles down like a car coasting to a stop. Train a GAN, and sometimes the two networks just... keep going. Not crashing, not converging — cycling.

cards.title: What 'oscillation' means here
cards.body: Training oscillation is when the generator's and discriminator's performance rise and fall in a repeating pattern instead of both steadily improving and settling into a stable equilibrium. Losses bounce; sample quality gets better, then worse, then better again.

cards.title: A cruise control that can't settle
cards.body: Imagine a cruise control system that overcorrects: it senses the car is a little slow, floors the accelerator, overshoots the target speed, slams the brakes, undershoots, floors it again — never settling on a steady speed. GAN training can fall into the same overcorrect-overcorrect loop, except the 'speed' is which network currently has the upper hand.

cards.title: Watching it happen over training
cards.body: Epoch 10: the generator's fakes look great, discriminator accuracy drops to 40%. Epoch 20: the discriminator catches up and crushes the generator, accuracy climbs to 95%. Epoch 30: the generator finds a new trick, discriminator accuracy crashes again. Round and round — with no epoch where things just calmly settle.

cards.title: More training epochs won't necessarily fix it
cards.body: It's tempting to think 'just train longer and it'll converge eventually,' the way most models do. But GAN training isn't guaranteed to converge just because you add more epochs — an oscillating pair of networks can keep chasing each other well past the point where a normal model would have leveled off.

cards.title: Typical training vs. GAN training
cards.body: How the loss behaves over time:

right.text:
Loss rises and falls
No flat landing
Can cycle indefinitely

exercises.prompt: What does it mean for GAN training to 'oscillate'?

options.text: Generator and discriminator performance repeatedly rise and fall instead of settling into a stable state
The loss resets to zero at the start of every epoch
The generator and discriminator take turns being turned off
Training speed alternates between fast and slow

exercises.explain: Oscillation means performance cycles up and down — not a steady march toward one stable point.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Because GANs are neural networks, more training epochs always guarantees the loss will eventually converge, just like a typical classifier.
exercises.explain: False — GAN training can oscillate indefinitely; extra epochs alone don't guarantee it'll settle down the way ordinary models typically do.

exercises.sentence: In many GAN training runs, the generator and discriminator's relative strength ___ back and forth instead of both steadily settling into a stable balance.
exercises.explain: The two networks' relative strength can swing back and forth — that's the oscillation.

exercises.prompt: Which loss-curve behavior is a hallmark of GAN training instability (oscillation), as opposed to typical convergence?

options.text: Discriminator accuracy climbs to 95%, crashes to 40%, climbs back to 90%, crashes again — repeating over many epochs
Loss drops for the first few epochs then stays flat near zero for the rest of training
Loss is high for one epoch then training stops

exercises.explain: Repeated rises and crashes over many epochs — with no settling — is the signature of oscillating GAN training.

exercises.prompt: You've just seen that GAN training can oscillate instead of settling down. If the generator's 'success' depends on fooling the discriminator, and the discriminator's 'success' depends on catching the generator, what would explain WHY neither one ever gets to just relax at a fixed target?

options.text: Because each network's target depends on the other network, and that other network keeps changing too — so the target itself keeps moving
Because the training data changes every epoch
Because computers can only calculate loss values for a limited number of epochs
Because generators and discriminators are not allowed to be evaluated at the same time

exercises.explain: Exactly — each network is chasing a target defined by the other network, which is also changing. Next up: that exact 'moving target' problem.
